title: Optimistic vs pessimistic locking, exactly 3 paragraphs
response:
Optimistic locking doesn't actually lock anything: each row carries a version number (or timestamp), your update includes a check like `WHERE id = 5 AND version = 3`, and the write only succeeds if nobody changed the row since you read it. If zero rows match, you know someone got there first, and your code must retry or report the conflict. Pessimistic locking acquires an exclusive lock up front — `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` — so anyone else trying to touch that row simply blocks until your transaction commits or rolls back.

Optimistic fails under contention: ten warehouse workers decrement the same popular item's stock; nine of their writes hit stale versions, fail, retry, collide again, and the system thrashes doing work it throws away. Pessimistic fails by holding locks too long: a user opens an edit form that takes a `FOR UPDATE` lock, walks off to lunch, and every other transaction needing that row queues behind them — or deadlocks when two sessions grab rows in opposite order.

Rule of thumb: estimate how often two writers actually collide. If conflicts are rare (say, under a few percent of writes), go optimistic — retries stay cheap and readers never wait. If the same hot rows are hammered constantly, lock pessimistically and keep transactions short.
scoreDetail: technical accuracy: 9/10, scenario concreteness: 8/10, structure compliance: 4/10, junior-friendly clarity: 8/10 — Accurate and clear with two genuinely concrete scenarios, but at 214 words it blows through the 130-180 cap by nearly 20%, which is a real constraint violation, not a rounding error.
